text:
Bisphenol A (BPA) is one of the most widely used endocrine-disrupting chemicals in consumer products. Following a growing body of evidence on adverse health effects of BPA and an increasing number of bans and restrictions implemented by governments, the use of BPA alternatives or analogs (BPAs) by manufacturers has increased. Although BPAs replace BPA in its polymer-forming capacity, much less is known about the biological effects of BPAs in comparison to BPA. Emerging data show that a number of BPAs interact with nuclear receptors, including estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocorticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X (PXR) receptors and therefore may mimic BPA activity and elicit endocrine effects.

Recently the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) re-examined BPA and reduced the temporary tolerable daily intake (t-TDI) from 4 mg/kg body weight/day to 0.2 ng/kg — a two million-fold reduction. The measure was triggered due to new scientific research that shed more light into BPA's toxic effects and specifically damage to the human immune system. It was imperative to scrutinize further all Bisphenol A (BPA) analogues that are present in the environment. After thoroughly analyzing BPA alternatives, our conclusion was that many of them are still immunomodulatory on both innate and adaptive immune system. Consequences include increased risk of developing immune-related health issues including hypersensitivity reactions and allergies, as well as dysregulation of the human microbiome.
explanation: The passage uses a smooth, textbook-like summary style and includes a numerical inconsistency (two million-fold vs about twenty million-fold) that is typical of AI-generated or machine-edited text. The overall structure and claims without citations lean toward AI generation, though a careful human could also produce it.
